question.text: Can an officer who has conducted an investigation or inspection in respect of an alleged violation be appointed as a designated authority under SEBI (Intermediaries) Regulations, 2008?

answer.text: Yes, but only if no other officer is available
No, such officer cannot be appointed as a designated authority
Yes, such officers are preferred due to their familiarity with the case
Yes, with approval from the Executive Director

explanation.text: **The Logic:** As per Section 6.4, no officer who has conducted an investigation or inspection in respect of the alleged violation can be appointed as a designated authority. This ensures impartiality and separation of investigation from adjudication. **The Trap:** Familiarity with the case may seem like an advantage, but the regulation explicitly prohibits this to prevent bias. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** This separation of roles is a fundamental principle of natural justice - the investigator cannot also be the judge.
